text: everywhere?” he asked . How long had he and Newton been talking? Lara had listened for a long time, asking questions when she couldn’t resist. Ronin was even older than he’d thought. He had existed in that long-ago, dead world, and she couldn’t help but wonder — had he cared for someone then like he did for Lara now ? Even through another Black Out, I could never forget you, Lara Brooks . But what if he’d forgotten someone else ? Exhaustion eventually caught up with her — helped along by the constant use of words she didn’t know or understand — and she had laid down on one of the bunks, drifting into a quick sleep while their voices continued behind her . “We had to assume so. There was no question that Denver suffered a direct strike. Fortunately — not that I can easily state anything was fortunate about the situation — the wind carried the fallout to the south…an oddity, considering that the prevailing wind typically blew from the south, at that time. Air current patterns have altered radically since then, of course, but—my apologies. I’ve a tendency to talk around my point . “There were no further communications, after Denver was destroyed .” “You said we .” Ronin glanced at Lara as she sat up and scooted beside him. He took her hand in his and turned his attention back to Newton . “I was assigned to the Air Force base in Cheyenne, working with William and his son during the war. Though portions of the city — specifically those in the vicinity of the freeway — were destroyed, there are heavily fortified facilities beneath the surface that weathered the worst of it. When I left, there were one hundred and nine personnel, civilian and military, surviving .” Lara’s brow creased. “What do you mean, beneath Cheyenne ?” Newton looked at her. His appearance didn’t seem so strange after seeing Ronin without skin. “Not beneath Cheyenne, Miss Brooks. Beneath Francis E. Warren Air Force Base, west of the city proper .” She raked her fingers through her tangled hair, pulling it out of her face. “There’s people there? Still ?” “It’s been many years since I was last there. Though they were, perhaps, limited on space, they were thriving .” There were people thriving , so close to Cheyenne? That meant they had food, secure shelter… She turned to Ronin. “Warlord doesn’t know they’re there !” “Warlord,” Newton echoed, curling one of his hands over the other. “I sometimes forget that’s what he calls himself. He suspected they were there, at the very least. Took his followers and scoured the entire surface of the base, searching for weapons and material, though there wasn’t much left after the war. They dismantled whatever they couldn’t take. Fortunately, his impatience won out long before he found a way down .” “You know him?” Ronin’s forehead creased . “As much as I would prefer to deny it, yes. I repaired and reactivated him. For a time, it seemed as though it would be to the benefit of many. He had such…vision. It is an uncommon trait for robots to possess so strongly. But…he was never like the rest of us .” “What do you mean?” Lara asked. “You mean he was always against humans ?” “Not at all , Miss — ” “Just Lara,” she said, cringing inwardly . “Lara, then. He had no more issue with humans than the rest of us, at least initially. Whatever information his data core retained through his deactivation must simply have left him with no conclusions to draw save that humans were responsible for what happened. Therefore, he does not believe them worthy of his trust or goodwill .” “Are there others like him out there ?” “I’ve never seen one like him,” Ronin said. “Bots and humans live separate in many places. Sometimes, with tension between them. But I’ve never been anyplace else where bots slaughtered humans, the way he has .” How many people had Warlord killed? Not just when the man in the attic had been alive, but in all the years after ? “To my knowledge,” Newton’s fingers fidgeted, “he is the only unit of his kind. He has a…unique means of processing that was still in an experimental phase during the war. Given more time, there would certainly have been more of his ilk. I think it is for the best, perhaps, that such research was ended .” “Experimental phase?” Lara drew her knees up and wrapped her arms around her legs. “Like how they changed Ronin ?” “There was nothing experimental in what was done to Ronin .” Ronin put an arm around Lara’s shoulders, and she leaned into him. Since the first time they made love, he’d been unable to keep his hands off her — as though he craved the physical contact as much as she did . “What, exactly, was done to me?” Ronin asked . “As I said, you were originally built to mimic a human. To be able to pass as one — or at least closely enough that the differences would be of little concern. When war erupted, and the scale of it increased, the supply of robots constructed specifically for military use was exhausted rapidly . “The governments of the world seized all the civilian units they could and repurposed them. You were one amongst hundreds of thousands. Your neural interface was upgraded to allow for increased reaction time, and they installed advanced optics and targeting systems. Your strength and speed inhibitors were disabled, allowing use of your full capabilities. And your casing was reinforced for increased durability. Synthetic humans were especially desirable for military applications, as the psychological effects of being unable to distinguish man from machine upon human combatants were significant over long periods of time .” “He was turned into a soldier,” Lara said . “Robots do not tire,” Newton explained. “We can react with greater speed
